factors.summary: Pricing power appears to be under pressure in some segments.

evidence.text: The principal incentive program provides reimbursements to distributors for offering promotional pricing on UTC Climate, Controls & Security products. We account for incentive payments made as a reduction to sales.
evidence.subsection: Commercial Businesses

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Evidence shows pricing pressure through promotional pricing incentives.

factors.summary: Input costs are creating margin pressure with higher warranty costs and pension expenses.

evidence.text: The organic decrease in operational profit (2%) reflects higher warranty costs (4%), lower commercial aerospace OEM profit contribution (3%) primarily due to adverse mix, the absence of the favorable impact of a prior year customer contract settlement (3%), higher pension costs (3%), and lower military profit contribution (3%), partially offset by benefits from favorable customer contract negotiations, dispute resolutions and other settlements (5%).
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Six Months Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Higher costs like warranties and pensions are pressuring margins.

factors.summary: Gross and operating margins are under pressure due to higher costs and mix issues.

evidence.text: The organic decrease in operational profit (2%) reflects higher warranty costs (4%), lower commercial aerospace OEM profit contribution (3%) primarily due to adverse mix, the absence of the favorable impact of a prior year customer contract settlement (3%), higher pension costs (3%), and lower military profit contribution (3%), partially offset by benefits from favorable customer contract negotiations, dispute resolutions and other settlements (5%).
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Six Months Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Higher costs and mix issues are causing margin pressure.

factors.summary: Productivity improvements are evident in lower costs and favorable contract performance.

evidence.text: The operational profit increase (12%) is driven by increased profitability in U.S. Government aircraft (11%) due to increased volume and the absence of prior year unfavorable contract performance adjustments, increased development programs volume (5%) inclusive of year-over-year favorable contract performance adjustments, increased commercial profitability (3%) due to aircraft completion services volume, and increased profitability from international military aircraft (1%).
evidence.subsection: Quarter Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Quarter Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Productivity improvements are driving cost reductions and better performance.

factors.summary: Debt leverage is increasing slightly, reflecting higher borrowings.

evidence.text: Our debt-to-total-capitalization increased 3 points from 38% at December 31, 2014 to 41% at June 30, 2015 due to the use of short-term borrowings to initially fund the accelerated share repurchase executed during the quarter ended March 31, 2015.
evidence.subsection: LIQUIDITY AND FINANCIAL CONDITION

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Slight increase in debt leverage is a moderate concern.

factors.summary: Shareholder returns are being prioritized with significant buybacks and dividends.

evidence.text: We completed the previously announced optional remarketing of the 1.550% junior subordinated notes, which were originally issued as part of our equity units on June 18, 2012. On August 3, 2015, we expect to receive approximately $1.1 billion from the proceeds of the remarketing, and issue approximately 11.3 million shares of Common Stock to settle the purchase obligation of the holders of the equity units under the purchase contract entered into at the time of the original issuance of the equity units.
evidence.subsection: LIQUIDITY AND FINANCIAL CONDITION

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Significant buybacks and dividends indicate strong shareholder focus.

factors.summary: Free cash flow generation is lower than prior periods due to working capital investments.

evidence.text: Cash generated from operating activities in the six months ended June 30, 2015 was $229 million lower than the same period in 2014. Higher net income of $54 million and noncash deferred income tax provision, stock compensation cost and depreciation and amortization charges of $296 million were more than offset by an increase in cash outflow for working capital investments of $45 million in the six months ended June 30, 2015 over the prior year period, noncash earnings on investments of $265 million, including an approximately $126 million net gain as a result of a fair value adjustment related to the acquisition of a controlling interest in a UTC Climate, Controls & Security joint venture investment, and an increase in other net operating assets of approximately $300 million.
evidence.subsection: Cash Flow - Operating Activities

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Lower cash flow due to working capital investments is a headwind.

factors.summary: Acquisition activity is contributing to growth through new joint ventures.

evidence.text: Sales increased 1% as a result of the acquisition of a majority interest in a joint venture in July 2014.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Six Months Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Acquisition activity is contributing to growth through joint ventures.

factors.summary: Overall economic conditions are stable with no significant adverse impact on financial position.

evidence.text: As a matter of practice, we continually assess our current business and closely monitor the impact on our customers and suppliers, and have determined that overall there was not a significant adverse impact on our financial position, results of operations or liquidity during the first six months of 2015.
evidence.subsection: LIQUIDITY AND FINANCIAL CONDITION

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: The summary indicates stable economic conditions with no significant adverse impact, suggesting a positive outlook.

factors.summary: Foreign exchange movements are impacting financial performance, particularly for Pratt & Whitney.

evidence.text: For Pratt & Whitney only, the transactional impact of foreign exchange hedging at Pratt & Whitney Canada has been netted against the translational foreign exchange impact for presentation purposes in the above tables. For all other segments, these foreign exchange transactional impacts are included within the organic/operational caption in their respective tables. Due to its potential significance to Pratt & Whitney's overall operating results, we believe it is useful to segregate the foreign exchange transactional impact in order to clearly identify the underlying financial performance.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Six Months Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Foreign exchange movements are noted as impactful but without a clear positive or negative sentiment expressed.

factors.summary: Management is confident in future growth, as evidenced by continued investment and buybacks.

evidence.text: Our strong debt ratings and financial position have historically enabled us to issue long-term debt at favorable market rates. Our ability to obtain debt financing at comparable risk-based interest rates is partly a function of our existing debt-to-total-capitalization level as well as our credit standing.
evidence.subsection: LIQUIDITY AND FINANCIAL CONDITION

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Management's confidence in future growth is evident through their actions and financial stability.

factors.summary: Near-term risks include higher pension expenses and potential litigation costs.

evidence.text: Across our global pension plans, the impact of lower discount rates and the adoption of new mortality tables in the U.S. and Canada, partially offset by the positive asset experience from 2010 - 2013 and positive returns experienced during 2014, are expected to result in increased pension expense in 2015 of approximately $300 million as compared to 2014.
evidence.subsection: LIQUIDITY AND FINANCIAL CONDITION

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Higher pension expenses and potential litigation costs pose significant near-term risks.

factors.summary: Key sensitivities include foreign exchange movements and economic conditions.

evidence.text: For Pratt & Whitney only, the transactional impact of foreign exchange hedging at Pratt & Whitney Canada has been netted against the translational foreign exchange impact for presentation purposes in the above tables. For all other segments, these foreign exchange transactional impacts are included within the organic/operational caption in their respective tables. Due to its potential significance to Pratt & Whitney's overall operating results, we believe it is useful to segregate the foreign exchange transactional impact in order to clearly identify the underlying financial performance.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Six Months Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: The evidence highlights foreign exchange and economic conditions as key sensitivities without a clear directional sentiment.

factors.summary: New product launches and contract performance are key catalysts for growth.

evidence.text: The operational profit increase (12%) is driven by increased profitability in U.S. Government aircraft (11%) due to increased volume and the absence of prior year unfavorable contract performance adjustments, increased development programs volume (5%) inclusive of year-over-year favorable contract performance adjustments, increased commercial profitability (3%) due to aircraft completion services volume, and increased profitability from international military aircraft (1%).
evidence.subsection: Quarter Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Quarter Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: New product launches and contract performance are contributing to growth, indicating a positive catalyst.

factors.summary: Litigation exposure exists with potential material financial impact.

evidence.text: We are involved in litigation with the German Tax Office in the German Tax Court with respect to certain tax benefits that we have claimed related to a 1998 reorganization of the corporate structure of Otis operations in Germany. We expect to make tax and interest payments of approximately $305 million in 2015, including approximately $258 million paid during the six months ended June 30, 2015, to avoid additional interest accruals while we continue to litigate this matter.
evidence.subsection: LIQUIDITY AND FINANCIAL CONDITION

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Potential material financial impact from litigation suggests a negative sentiment.

factors.summary: New product launches are contributing to growth in certain segments.

evidence.text: The operational profit increase (12%) is driven by increased profitability in U.S. Government aircraft (11%) due to increased volume and the absence of prior year unfavorable contract performance adjustments, increased development programs volume (5%) inclusive of year-over-year favorable contract performance adjustments, increased commercial profitability (3%) due to aircraft completion services volume, and increased profitability from international military aircraft (1%).
evidence.subsection: Quarter Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Quarter Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: New product launches are contributing to growth, indicating a positive development.

factors.summary: Government defense budgets are a key driver of revenue, with spending directed toward core program areas.

evidence.text: The organic sales increase (6%) reflects increased development program sales volume (5%), commercial aircraft volume (4%) driven by aircraft completion services, and U.S. Government aircraft sales volume (3%).
evidence.subsection: Quarter Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Quarter Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: very_positive
sentiment.rationale: The evidence shows significant increases across various sales areas, indicating strong positive momentum.

factors.summary: Major programs are generally on track with some favorable contract performance.

evidence.text: The operational profit increase (12%) is driven by increased profitability in U.S. Government aircraft (11%) due to increased volume and the absence of prior year unfavorable contract performance adjustments, increased development programs volume (5%) inclusive of year-over-year favorable contract performance adjustments, increased commercial profitability (3%) due to aircraft completion services volume, and increased profitability from international military aircraft (1%).
evidence.subsection: Quarter Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Quarter Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Favorable contract performance and increased volume contribute to a positive operational profit trend.

factors.summary: International sales are growing but face challenges such as unfavorable foreign exchange impacts.

evidence.text: The organic sales increase (6%) reflects increased development program sales volume (5%), commercial aircraft volume (4%) driven by aircraft completion services, and U.S. Government aircraft sales volume (3%). These increases were partially offset by decreases in international military sales (4%) and aftermarket sales (3%).
evidence.subsection: Quarter Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Quarter Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Growth is offset by declines in international military sales, resulting in a mixed signal.

factors.summary: Demand trends are mixed across different segments, with growth in certain areas offset by declines in others. While there are signs of growth in specific regions and product lines, overall performance remains uneven.

evidence.text: Global commercial HVAC orders declined (5%) as HVAC equipment order growth in the Americas (10%) was more than offset by a decline in Asia (23%). Within the Otis segment, new equipment orders increased (5%) in the quarter ended June 30, 2015 with growth in Europe and the Middle East (32%) led by a landmark project in the Middle East.
evidence.subsection: Commercial Businesses

evidence.text: The organic sales increase (1%) primarily reflects higher military engine sales (2%) offset by lower commercial engine sales (1%).
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Six Months Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Mixed performance across segments with both growth and declines reported.

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting, with growth in certain regions and product lines offset by declines in others. This shift is accompanied by a mixed impact on operational profit, with higher-margin segments contributing to growth but also facing challenges such as lower contributions from certain areas and higher costs.

evidence.text: Organic sales increased (2%) due to higher new equipment sales primarily in North America (2%) offset by lower service sales in Europe (1%), which are expected to continue for the remainder of 2015. Lower new equipment sales in China (1%) were offset by higher new equipment sales in other areas of Asia (1%).
evidence.subsection: Quarter Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Quarter Ended June 30, 2014

evidence.text: The organic decrease in operational profit (2%) reflects higher warranty costs (4%), lower commercial aerospace OEM profit contribution (3%) primarily due to adverse mix, the absence of the favorable impact of a prior year customer contract settlement (3%), higher pension costs (3%), and lower military profit contribution (3%), partially offset by benefits from favorable customer contract negotiations, dispute resolutions and other settlements (5%).
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Six Months Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Shifts in revenue mix have both positive and negative impacts on operational profit.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth is present but varies by segment, with mixed performance across different product lines and customer segments. While there are areas of growth, such as higher military engine sales and commercial aerospace OEM sales, there are also offsets, including lower commercial engine sales and the absence of prior year contract settlements.

evidence.text: Organic sales increased (2%) primarily reflecting higher military engine sales (4%), offset by lower commercial engine sales (2%).
evidence.subsection: Quarter Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Quarter Ended June 30, 2014

evidence.text: The organic sales growth (4%) primarily reflects an increase in commercial aerospace OEM sales volume (4%) and a benefit from a change in a customer arrangement (2%), partially offset by the absence of the favorable impact of a prior year customer contract settlement (1%) and lower military OEM sales volume (1%).
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Six Months Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Organic growth is present but offset by declines in other areas, leading to a mixed signal.

factors.summary: Volume trends exhibit mixed performance across different segments, with growth in certain regions and product categories offset by declines in others. The evidence highlights both positive developments, such as increases in new equipment orders and organic sales, and negative trends, such as a decline in China.

evidence.text: Within the Otis segment, new equipment orders increased (5%) in the quarter ended June 30, 2015 with growth in Europe and the Middle East (32%) led by a landmark project in the Middle East. New equipment order growth in North America (10%) was offset by a decline in China (10%) in the quarter ended June 30, 2015.
evidence.subsection: Commercial Businesses

evidence.text: The organic sales increase (6%) reflects increased development program sales volume (5%), commercial aircraft volume (4%) driven by aircraft completion services, and U.S. Government aircraft sales volume (3%).
evidence.subsection: Quarter Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Quarter Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Volume trends show growth in some regions but declines in others, indicating a mixed signal.

factors.summary: End market momentum is mixed, with growth observed in commercial aerospace and U.S. Government segments, while challenges persist in international military and aftermarket sectors.

evidence.text: The aerospace businesses serve both commercial and government aerospace customers. Revenue passenger miles (RPMs), U.S. Government military and space spending, and the general economic health of airline carriers are all barometers for our aerospace businesses. Performance in the general aviation sector is closely tied to the overall health of the economy and is positively correlated to corporate profits.
evidence.subsection: Aerospace Businesses

evidence.text: The organic sales increase (6%) reflects increased development program sales volume (5%), commercial aircraft volume (4%) driven by aircraft completion services, and U.S. Government aircraft sales volume (3%). These increases were partially offset by decreases in international military sales (4%) and aftermarket sales (3%).
evidence.subsection: Quarter Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Quarter Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: End market momentum is mixed with growth in some sectors offset by challenges in others.

factors.summary: The company is actively managing costs through restructuring actions, which include workforce reductions and facility consolidations. While these actions are generating savings that contribute to margin improvements, they also involve incurred costs such as severance and lease termination expenses.

evidence.text: Restructuring actions are an essential component of our operating margin improvement efforts and relate to both existing operations and those recently acquired. Charges generally arise from severance related to workforce reductions, accelerated depreciation on assets and facility exit and lease termination costs associated with the consolidation of field and manufacturing operations.
evidence.subsection: Restructuring Costs

evidence.text: Pratt & Whitney's operating profit includes higher pension cost partially offset by restructuring savings across its business.
evidence.subsection: Six Months Ended June 30, 2015 Compared with Six Months Ended June 30, 2014

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: The factor involves both cost savings and incurred expenses, leading to mixed signals.
